raw_case:
An Odd Presentation of Dysphagia Due to Pericardial Effusion
Esophageal dysphagia is most commonly caused by motility disorders and intrinsic mechanical obstruction. However, extrinsic obstruction, such as pericardial effusion, is rare causes of dysphagia. We present an 89-year-old male with history of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome, and basal cell carcinoma presenting with generalized weakness, productive cough, shortness of breath, and dysphagia to both solids and liquids. A chest X-ray obtained showed cardiomegaly with suggested central vascular congestion and pulmonary edema. Further imaging with computed tomography (CT) abdomen and pelvis showed a moderate-to-large pericardial effusion. Patient later developed signs and symptoms of cardiac tamponade, requiring urgent pericardiocentesis with removal of 1 L of sanguineous fluid. Up to today, only 6 cases of dysphagia due to pericardial effusion have been described. This case displays another rare case and highlights the importance of recognizing dysphagia as a critical symptom as well as non-gastrointestinal (GI) causes of dysphagia.

Introduction

Pericardial effusion is a serious cardiac condition that involves the accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac that surmounts the normal amount of 15 to 50 mL of fluid.
1
 The most common causes include acute pericarditis, autoimmune disease, trauma, postmyocardial infarction, or cardiac surgery, malignancy, radiation, and uremia.1-3 The accumulation of fluid may be rapid, resulting in an acute presentation, or more gradual, resulting in a subacute or chronic presentation.1,2 Signs and symptoms are non-specific. However, symptoms may include dyspnea, hoarseness, coughing, chest pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms via stimulation of the Vagus nerve and diaphragm.1,4 Once intrapericardial pressure rises to a level that is severe enough to impair chamber filling, cardiac tamponade ensues, with signs and symptoms including jugular vein distention (JVD), sinus tachycardia, hypotension, pulsus paradoxus, low voltage QRS on electrocardiogram (EKG), and muffled heart sounds.1,4,5 In this report, we present a case of subacute pericardial effusion and highlight dysphagia as an acute critical finding.

Case Presentation

An 89-year-old male with history of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome, basal cell carcinoma, and hypertension (HTN) presented to the emergency department (ED) with complaints of general weakness and shortness of breath with productive cough of green phlegm that started about 3 weeks ago. Patient also reported orthopnea, dyspnea on exertion, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. His granddaughter stated that his clinical symptoms improved after 2 weeks, but started to worsen again 1 week later. Patient visited his oncologist, who prescribed Levofloxacin for upper respiratory infection. Since then, patient has not been able to eat or drink, reporting intermittent coughing with liquids and emesis with any oral intake. Denied any fever, chills, chest pain, headache, nausea, or abdominal pain.On presentation to the ED, patient had a temperature of 98.1°F, heart rate 90 beats/minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths/minute, blood pressure (BP) of 140/65 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Physical examination was remarkable for 2+ pitting edema of the bilateral lower extremities, swelling over the bilateral flanks, and ecchymosis of the upper extremities. Cardiovascular examination revealed an irregular rhythm but no murmurs, rubs, distant heart sounds, JVD, or pulsus paradoxus.Initial laboratory values were remarkable for a white blood cell count of 7.9 × 103/µL (baseline 3-4 × 103/µL), hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL, platelets 130 × 103/µL, K 5.9 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 60 mg/dL, Cr 3.62 mg/dL (baseline 2.0 mg/dL), HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactic acid 3.2 mmol/L, and albumin 2.4 g/dL. The EKG obtained (Figure 1) was significant for atrial flutter with variable AV block but not low-voltage QRS or electrical alternans. Chest X-ray (Figure 2) showed cardiomegaly with suggested central venous congestion, pulmonary edema, and possible small effusions. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast (Figure 3) showed moderate-to-large pericardial effusion, small-to-moderate bilateral pleural effusions, mild diffuse increased soft tissue density throughout the mesentery, and prominent lymph nodes. While in the ED, patient was given calcium gluconate 1 g and insulin 5 units for his hyperkalemia, as well as bicarbonate 50 mEq push and 50 mL D50W for his acute kidney injury. Cardiology was consulted for further work-up and possible intervention.A transthoracic echocardiogram obtained showed a normal ejection fraction at 60% and confirmed a large pericardial effusion without tamponade along with pleural effusion. The day after admission, the patient became hypotensive with mean arterial pressures (MAPs) in the high 50s. Physical examination also revealed distant heart sounds and decreased breath sounds with crackles, with concern for tamponade. Urgent pericardiocentesis was performed with drainage of 1 L of hemorrhagic fluid and sent for cytology. Patient’s hypotension immediately resolved. His dyspnea and weakness improved the following day. Patient no longer complained of dysphagia and was able to tolerate oral feeds. Cytology was negative for any neoplastic cells. A repeat echocardiogram performed several days after pericardiocentesis showed mild residual pericardial effusion. Patient was discharged in stable condition and on appropriate medications.

Discussion

Pericardial effusions can be seen in all ages and populations. The most common causes are infection, causing 16% to 27%, malignancy, causing 13% to 23%, and iatrogenic, causing 13% to 18%.
2
 Within the developed world, viral pericarditis is the leading cause of effusion, whereas in developing areas, it is Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
3
 Of malignancies, lymphoma and breast are the leading causes.
2
 In the acute setting, only 100 to 150 mL of fluid is needed to cause tamponade. On the contrary, up to 1 to 2 L of fluid can accumulate in the chronic setting before tamponade ensues as the heart has adequate time to accommodate the increase in volume. In both cases, once the fluid level reaches a point that causes compression of the heart and impairs filling, tamponade develops with a progressive decline in cardiac output and blood pressure.1,3,4 Symptoms of pericardial effusion are non-specific, but include dyspnea (due to lung compression and atelectasis), hoarseness (due to compression of the recurrent laryngeal nerve), coughing, chest pain, and dysphagia (from esophageal compression).4,5 Of those with cardiac tamponade, only a small amount present with the classic Beck’s triad.
3
 Other signs and symptoms of tamponade include low-voltage QRS complexes, electrical alternans, and pulsus paradoxus.
2
In our case, the patient presented with a subacute pericardial effusion of about 1 L with several non-specific symptoms including dyspnea, cough, and dysphagia that eventually led to tamponade associated with distant heart sounds, decreased breath sounds, and persistent hypotension. It is most likely that our patient’s effusion developed over the span of several weeks considering his complaints of dyspnea, weakness that began 3 weeks prior. Owing to the gradual progression of the effusion, the patient’s heart was able to accommodate the slow accumulation of fluid without compromise until the maximum level of fluid resulted in compression and decompensation.The study of choice for pericardial effusion is an echocardiogram, either transthoracic or transesophageal, as it establishes the diagnosis and guides management.3,4 It allows for evaluation for the size of the effusion and whether tamponade is present.1,3,4 It appears as an anechoic area surrounding the heart.3,4 The size of the fluid in diastole is used to grade the effusion.
3
 In our case, the echocardiogram was able to identify a moderate-to-large effusion.The treatment for pericardial effusion depends on its size and etiology. It may include watching for small effusions without hemodynamic compromise with sequential echocardiograms to diagnostic and therapeutic pericardiocentesis for moderate-to-large effusions or symptomatic patients. Emergent treatment is necessary for patients presenting with hemodynamic instability.1,3 In our case, our patient presented with symptoms and a moderate-to-large effusion. However, due to development of instability, urgent pericardiocentesis was performed, with removal of about 1 L of hemorrhagic fluid. Cytology was negative for any malignant cells.Although pericardial effusion is a known cause esophageal dysphagia, it is rare.6,7 Prior reports of dysphagia due to left atrium enlargement and mediastinal masses have been documented; however, only a few due to pericardial effusion have thus far been.4,7 Among individuals greater than 50 years of age, the estimated prevalence of dysphagia is 16% to 22%.
7
 Causes of esophageal dysphagia can be broken down into 2 categories, which include motility dysfunction or mechanical obstruction. Of the causes of mechanical obstruction, intrinsic lesions such as strictures, masses, rings, or webs are the most common caused.7,8 Our patient presented with a week of dysphagia associated with intermittent coughing with liquids and emesis with any oral intake.In the case that we present, the acute onset of dysphagia highlights the importance of recognizing dysphagia as a critical symptom of pericardial effusion. Although a non-specific symptom, recognizing its presence may aid identifying the size of the effusion prior to echocardiogram, as well as guide management of the effusion. However, we cannot definitively say this as only a handful of cases has been reported. Furthermore, it emphasizes that even though a small percentage of dysphagia are non–GI-related, they are crucial causes that should not be missed. For this reason, one must obtain a detailed history and characterize the symptoms to identify the cause.

Conclusion

Pericardial effusion is a rare but serious cause of acute dysphagia. In our patient, dysphagia developed 1 week prior to presentation, with the eventual development of tamponade and resolution of symptoms after pericardiocentesis. Given this presentation, the presence of dysphagia in those with pericardial effusion may warn of a large effusion and the potential for development of cardiac tamponade.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 89-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Generalized weakness, productive cough, shortness of breath, and dysphagia to solids and liquids.
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms of dyspnea, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea started three weeks prior. Condition initially improved with prescribed levofloxacin for a suspected upper respiratory infection but worsened after one week, leading to dysphagia, intermittent coughing with liquids, and emesis with oral intake.
- **Past Medical History:** Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome, basal cell carcinoma, and hypertension.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Significant for 2+ pitting edema in lower extremities, flank swelling, ecchymosis on upper extremities, irregular cardiovascular rhythm without murmurs or JVD, and no signs of pulsus paradoxus.
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **Chest X-ray:** Cardiomegaly, central venous congestion, pulmonary edema, small effusions.  
  - **CT abdomen/pelvis:** Moderate-to-large pericardial effusion, bilateral pleural effusions, and prominent lymph nodes.  
  - **EKG:** Atrial flutter with variable AV block, no low-voltage QRS or electrical alternans.  
  - **Echocardiogram:** Large pericardial effusion, no tamponade initially.  
  - **Laboratory findings:** Elevated potassium, elevated BUN/Cr, low bicarbonate and albumin, and mild anemia; cytology of pericardial fluid negative for neoplastic cells.
  - **Post-pericardiocentesis echocardiogram:** Mild residual pericardial effusion.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Esophageal Dysphagia Causes:**
   - Dysphagia can be due to intrinsic mechanical obstruction (e.g., strictures, masses) or motility dysfunction. Excluded via history and imaging (no masses or strictures).
   - Extrinsic mechanical causes considered, supported by imaging findings of pericardial effusion.

2. **Pericardial Effusion:**
   - Confirmed by echocardiography and CT. Dysphagia attributed to esophageal compression from a large effusion. Other systemic symptoms like dyspnea, orthopnea, and weakness align with this diagnosis.
   - Cardiac tamponade ruled out initially but developed later based on hypotension, distant heart sounds, and decreased breath sounds.

3. **Infections and Malignancy:**
   - Despite the history of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, cytology was negative for malignancy, making malignancy-related effusion unlikely.
   - No confirmatory findings for infection-related causes of pericardial effusion.

4. **Other Possible Causes:**
   - Autoimmune, uremia, and other causes of pericardial effusion were considered but deprioritized based on clinical and laboratory findings.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Pericardial effusion leading to extrinsic esophageal compression and subsequent dysphagia, with progression to cardiac tamponade. Resolved with therapeutic pericardiocentesis.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Pericardial effusion causing dysphagia and subsequent cardiac tamponade.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

I'm going to walk through this patient case step by step to figure out what's going on. The patient is an 89-year-old male with a complex medical history, and he's presenting with a variety of symptoms that seem to be interconnected in some way.

First, let's look at his chief complaints: generalized weakness, productive cough, shortness of breath, and dysphagia to solids and liquids. These are pretty broad symptoms that could point to a number of different issues, so I need to consider how they might relate to each other and to his past medical history.

The history of present illness indicates that his symptoms of dyspnea, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea started three weeks before presentation. Initially, these were attributed to an upper respiratory infection, and he was treated with levofloxacin, which seemed to improve his condition at first but then worsened after one week, leading to new symptoms like dysphagia, coughing with liquids, and vomiting with oral intake.

Given his age and medical history, including Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome, basal cell carcinoma, and hypertension, there are several potential underlying issues that could be contributing to his current state.

Let's start by considering his respiratory symptoms: dyspnea, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. These are classic signs of heart failure, particularly left-sided heart failure leading to pulmonary congestion and edema. The initial improvement with antibiotic therapy suggests that there may have been a superimposed infection, possibly exacerbating his underlying heart condition.

However, the subsequent development of dysphagia and issues with oral intake suggests that there might be another process at play, possibly involving the esophagus or even neurological dysfunction given his history of Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome.

Now, looking at the physical examination findings: 2+ pitting edema in lower extremities, flank swelling, ecchymosis on upper extremities, irregular cardiovascular rhythm without murmurs or JVD, and no signs of pulsus paradoxus.

The edema and flank swelling could indicate fluid overload, consistent with heart failure or kidney dysfunction. The ecchymosis might suggest a coagulopathy or vascular fragility, which could be related to his age or possibly to his Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, given that this condition can affect platelet function and increase bleeding tendency.

The irregular rhythm without murmurs or JVD suggests an arrhythmia, possibly atrial fibrillation or flutter, which is also consistent with the EKG finding of atrial flutter with variable AV block.

Moving on to the ancillary tests:

- Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly, central venous congestion, pulmonary edema, and small effusions. This strongly supports the diagnosis of heart failure, likely congestive heart failure with pulmonary involvement.

- CT abdomen/pelvis reveals a moderate-to-large pericardial effusion, bilateral pleural effusions, and prominent lymph nodes. The pericardial effusion is particularly concerning as it could potentially lead to cardiac tamponade, which would be a life-threatening condition requiring urgent intervention.

- EKG shows atrial flutter with variable AV block, no low-voltage QRS or electrical alternans. The atrial flutter can contribute to heart failure by leading to atrial systole being lost, reducing cardiac output.

- Echocardiogram shows a large pericardial effusion but no evidence of tamponade initially. This is important because tamponade can present with signs like pulsus paradoxus, which was not noted in the physical exam.

- Laboratory findings include elevated potassium, elevated BUN/Cr, low bicarbonate and albumin, and mild anemia. These could indicate renal insufficiency, possibly due to dehydration or heart failure affecting renal perfusion. The cytology of pericardial fluid being negative for neoplastic cells is reassuring regarding the cause of the pericardial effusion.

- Post-pericardiocentesis echocardiogram shows a mild residual pericardial effusion, suggesting that the procedure was successful in reducing the effusion.

Now, considering all these findings, I need to synthesize them into a coherent diagnosis.

The patient has a history of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, which is a type of lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma characterized by increased production of monoclonal IgM immunoglobulins. This condition can affect multiple organs and systems, including the heart and kidneys.

Given that the pericardial fluid cytology was negative for neoplastic cells, it's less likely that the pericardial effusion is directly due to Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, but it's still possible, as sometimes malignant cells may not be detected in fluid analysis.

Alternatively, the pericardial effusion could be due to other causes such as uremic pericarditis, given the elevated BUN and creatinine levels, or it could be a complication of the heart failure itself.

The dysphagia and issues with oral intake could be related to esophageal dysfunction, possibly due to neurological impairment from Charcot-Marie-Tooth syndrome, which is a peripheral nerve disorder. However, dysphagia in an elderly patient with heart failure and respiratory symptoms could also be due to heartburn or aspiration, given the pulmonary congestion.

Given the complexity of this case, with multiple systems involved and a history of malignancy, I need to consider whether there is an underlying systemic process driving these symptoms.

First, the heart failure seems to be a significant component, given the chest X-ray and echocardiogram findings. The atrial flutter is likely contributing to the heart failure by reducing the efficiency of heart contractions.

Second, the pericardial effusion is a critical finding that could complicate the heart failure if it leads to tamponade. Although initial echocardiogram did not show tamponade, the presence of a large effusion necessitated pericardiocentesis, which was performed and reduced the effusion to mild residual.

Third, the renal dysfunction, as evidenced by elevated BUN and creatinine, could be due to reduced renal perfusion from heart failure or possibly from dehydration, although the edema suggests fluid overload rather than dehydration.

Fourth, the dysphagia and oral intake issues need to be addressed, as they could lead to malnutrition and further complications. Given the patient's age and comorbidities, a thorough evaluation of the esophagus, possibly with a barium swallow or endoscopy, might be warranted to assess for structural abnormalities or motility disorders.

Finally, considering the patient's age and the complexity of his medical history, a multidisciplinary approach may be necessary, involving cardiology, nephrology, gastroenterology, and possibly oncology, to manage all aspects of his care.

In summary, the primary diagnoses appear to be:

1. Congestive heart failure with pulmonary edema, likely exacerbated by atrial flutter.

2. Large pericardial effusion, managed with pericardiocentesis.

3. Renal insufficiency possibly due to heart failure-induced reduced perfusion.

4. Dysphagia, etiology to be determined, possibly related to esophageal or neurological issues.

Given the patient's age and comorbidities, management should focus on symptom relief, optimization of heart failure therapy, monitoring for recurrence of pericardial effusion, and ensuring adequate nutrition and hydration despite dysphagia.

### Answer:

Congestive Heart Failure with Pulmonary Edema, Atrial Flutter, Large Pericardial Effusion, and Dysphagia of Unknown Etiology

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Dysphagia can result from intrinsic mechanical obstruction or motility dysfunction, but these were excluded based on history and imaging findings showing no masses or strictures.
<Step 2> Extrinsic mechanical causes of dysphagia were considered, supported by imaging findings of pericardial effusion.

<Step 3> Pericardial effusion was confirmed by echocardiography and CT, and dysphagia was attributed to esophageal compression from the large effusion, with systemic symptoms like dyspnea, orthopnea, and weakness aligning with this diagnosis.
<Step 4> Cardiac tamponade was initially ruled out but later developed, evidenced by hypotension, distant heart sounds, and decreased breath sounds.
<Step 5> Malignancy-related pericardial effusion was deemed unlikely due to negative cytology findings despite a history of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia.
<Step 6> Infection-related causes of pericardial effusion were considered but lacked confirmatory findings.
<Step 7> Autoimmune, uremia, and other causes of pericardial effusion were deprioritized based on clinical and laboratory findings.
<Step 8> The final diagnosis was pericardial effusion causing extrinsic esophageal compression and dysphagia, with progression to cardiac tamponade.
<Step 9> The condition was resolved with therapeutic pericardiocentesis.
